coredata.dc:title: Energy efficient room temperature synthesis of cardanol-based novolac resin using acoustic cavitation
coredata.dc:creator: Jadhav, Nilesh L.
Sastry, Sai Krishna C.
Pinjari, Dipak V.
coredata.dc:description: The present study deals with synthesis of cardanol-cased novolac (CBN) resin by the condensation reaction between cardanol and formaldehyde using acoustic cavitation. It is a step-growth polymerization which occurs in the presence of an acid catalyst such as adipic acid, citric acid, oxalic acid, sulphuric acid and hydrochloric acid. CBN was also synthesised by a conventional method for the sake of comparison of techniques. The effect of molar ratio, effect of catalyst, effect of different catalyst and effect of power on the conversion to CBN has been studied. The synthesised CBN was characterized using the Fourier Transform Infra Red Spectroscopy (FTIR), Gel Permeation Chromatography (GPC), Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) Spectroscopy and Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA). The reaction was monitored by the Acid value, free formaldehyde content and viscosity of the synthesised product. The reaction time required for the conventionally synthesised CBN was 5 h (300 min) with 120 °C as an operating temperature while sonochemically the time reduced to 30 min at room temperature. The amount of time and energy saved can be quantified. Ultrasound facilitated synthesis was found to be an energy efficient and time-saving method for the synthesis of novolac resin.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasound
Cardanol-based novolac resin
Room temperature

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CBN resin using conventional heating method (NUS)
Synthesis of CBN resin using ultrasound irradiation method (US)
Optimisation of cardanol to formaldehyde ratio
Optimisation of cardanol to catalyst ratio
Effect of different catalysts on the CBN
Effect of power on the CBN

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental Set-up of the acoustic cavitation (Ultrasonic Horn).
Effect of time on Acid value and free formaldehyde content of the synthesised CBN using NUS method.
Variation of pH with respect to time of the CBN synthesised using US method.
Effect of time on the free formaldehyde content (%) and Acid Value of the CBN synthesised using US method.
Effect of time on the Viscosity (cP) of the CBN synthesised using US method.
Effect of molar ratio of the reactants on the viscosity of the CBN synthesised using US method.
Effect of catalyst loading on the viscosity and pH of the CBN synthesised using US method.
Effect of acid catalysts on the viscosity of the CBN synthesised using US method.
FTIR spectrum of ultrasonically synthesised CBN resin.
GPC Chromatogram of the CBN synthesised using US method.
1H NMR Spectroscopy of the CBN synthesised using US method.
TGA of the uncured CBN synthesised using US method.
Optimisation of energy of the ultrasonic horn on the basis of viscosity of the CBN synthesised sing US method.
Parameters obtained from GPC of the CBN synthesised using US method.

head.ce:title: Energy efficient room temperature synthesis of cardanol-based novolac resin using acoustic cavitation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Institute of Chemical Technology, N.P. Marg, Matunga, Mumbai 400 019, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study deals with synthesis of cardanol-cased novolac (CBN) resin by the condensation reaction between cardanol and formaldehyde using acoustic cavitation. It is a step-growth polymerization which occurs in the presence of an acid catalyst such as adipic acid, citric acid, oxalic acid, sulphuric acid and hydrochloric acid. CBN was also synthesised by a conventional method for the sake of comparison of techniques. The effect of molar ratio, effect of catalyst, effect of different catalyst and effect of power on the conversion to CBN has been studied. The synthesised CBN was characterized using the Fourier Transform Infra Red Spectroscopy (FTIR), Gel Permeation Chromatography (GPC), Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) Spectroscopy and Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA). The reaction was monitored by the Acid value, free formaldehyde content and viscosity of the synthesised product. The reaction time required for the conventionally synthesised CBN was 5 h (300 min) with 120 °C as an operating temperature while sonochemically the time reduced to 30 min at room temperature. The amount of time and energy saved can be quantified. Ultrasound facilitated synthesis was found to be an energy efficient and time-saving method for the synthesis of novolac resin.

ce:section.ce:para: In the process of removal of cashew kernel from its nut, an oily liquid is obtained as a byproduct namely Cashew Nut Shell Liquid (CSNL). CSNL is a natural renewable source and its production is in million tons [1–3]. CSNL mainly contains the anacardic acid, cardanol and cardol. There is a lot of heat generated in the mechanical extraction of CNSL leading to decarboxylation of anacardic acid to cardanol; therefore the main constituent of technical CSNL is cardanol along with lack amounts of methylcardol and cardol [2]. Cardanol has some interesting properties leading to a vast spectrum of applications in the polymer industry; some of its properties are high electrical insulation, good thermal stability and quick drying post-baking. Cardanol is basically a phenolic based moiety annexed with a C15 chain in the meta position; the long aliphatic chain is a mixture of unsaturated (mono, di and tri) and saturated compounds and can be polymerized in many ways [4,5].

The reaction between Cardanol and formaldehyde in presence of acid, it forms Cardanol-Based Novolac (CBN) resin, which is quite similar to the formation of novolac resin from phenol and formaldehyde [6]. Novolac resin is synthesised using phenol and formaldehyde with an excess of phenol; it is an acid catalysed condensation reaction. The internal plasticization present in the CBN resin (effect of C15 chain of cardanol) makes it more flexible than conventional novolac resin; also the long side chain instils hydrophobicity to the CBN resin, making it resistant to weathering and repellant to water [7]. Apart from having few advantages over conventional novolac; a vital factor is that cardanol is obtained from CNSL, which is a renewable source whereas phenol is a fossil fuel derived monomer [8]. In the period of fuel crisis, cognizance dwells in usage of more and more monomers which are extracted from renewable sources. The applications of CBN have been proposed in the composites [9], reinforcements [10], etc. It can be also used as a modifier to epoxy resin [11].

Recently, some of researchers have shown interest in synthesis of polymer with help of ultrasound [12,13]. Ultrasound covers the frequency range which lies beyond the audible range (20 kHz–5 MHz). During various stages of irradiation, ultrasonic waves propagate into liquid as rarefaction and compression cycles. In this process of cavitation, bubbles or cavities are generated. These bubbles grow subsequently and reach a maximum size and ultimately collapse in a violent manner leading to significant local energy dissipation [14]. When the wave propagates into the liquid, there is an achievement of immense temperature and pressure at the local hot spots, of the order of 4000–10,000 K and 1.013 × 108 Pa respectively [15,16]. Hot spots and free radicals generated during cavitation can impart chemical effects to the reaction improvising the overall reaction process where as intense turbulence generated by the ultrasonic waves improve the mass transfer rates and catalyst effectiveness [17,18]. With regard to polymer synthesis ultrasound aids to accomplish the results at lower temperatures, faster reaction times, better polydispersity index and controlled molecular weight [19,20].

Some of the previous studies encompass polymerization of styrene, acrylic acid, acrylamide and acrylonitrile [21–23]; one common point among all these illustrations is chain-growth polymerization. It is mainly the chemical effects of ultrasound that favor the chain growth polymerization. At lower irradiation intensities of ultrasound or acoustic cavitation leads to the release of OH and H radicals which in turn hasten up the process of chain growth polymerization. At higher intensities of ultrasound, physical effects dominate which is appropriate for step-growth polymerization. Meagre literature is available wherein ultrasound has been used for step-growth polymerization, some of the previous studies discussed the use of high-intensity ultrasound to synthesise step-growth polymers. These include the synthesis of aromatic polyformals [24], synthesis of polyurethanes [25], polymerization of small cyclic polycarbonate oligomers by using high-intensity ultrasound [26], polymerization of cyclic siloxanes to silicone resins [27] and some other polymers. Synthesising novolac resins from phenol and formaldehyde is very well recognised and commercialised process; use of cardanol instead of phenol is a newer concept whereas the use of ultrasound to synthesise CBN resin is an untouched area.

In this study, CBN resin have synthesised using the acoustic cavitation (Ultrasonic Horn). The synthesised product was subjected to physical and chemical analysis. Parameters were optimised based on the acid value, free formaldehyde content and the developed viscosity. The product was analysed by using Fourier Transform Infra Red (FTIR) Spectroscopy, Gel Permeation Chromatography (GPC), Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) Spectroscopy and Thermo Gravimetric Analysis (TGA). A simple protocol has been developed for the synthesis of cardanol based resin (CBN).

Cardanol of 80% purity was kindly supplied by Gargi Chemicals Limited (Mumbai). Formaldehyde (37% v/w), hydrochloric acid (37%), sulphuric acid (98%), methanol (>98%), and NMR grade deuterated chloroform (CDCl3, 99.8 atom% D) were obtained from Thomas Baker (Mumbai). Catalysts including citric acid, adipic acid, oxalic acid utilised in the experiments were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Private Limited (Mumbai) and had purity more than 98%.
R = C15H31-n
Reaction mechanism for the synthesis of CBN

In experimental method, the ultrasonic horn used was of Dakshin India Ltd (Fig. 1 ). The ultrasonic horn had a frequency of 20 kHz and power of 200 W. Ultrasonic horn was fixed in the reaction mixture dipped to a depth of 15 mm. Magnetic stirrer was used for stirring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CBN resin using conventional heating method (NUS)
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the synthesis of novolac resin was carried out using conventional heating method with help of previously published data [4,8], in order to compare the ultrasonically synthesised product with the conventionally synthesised one. For the conventional reaction, cardanol and formaldehyde were taken in the molar ratios of 1:0.5. Citric acid (2.5% of the weight of the cardanol taken) was used as a catalyst. Citric acid was dissolved in 5 ml of methanol. Half of the catalyst solution was added to formaldehyde (7.32 g) and half of it was added to cardanol. Cardanol (60 g) and catalyst solution were mixed in a 500 ml three-necked round bottom flask using magnetic stirrer. The flask was kept in an oil bath; it was equipped with a thermometer and Liebig’s condenser. The magnetic needle was used for stirring action and the reaction was maintained under a nitrogen atmosphere. The system was preheated to 65 °C and then the solution of formaldehyde and catalyst was added dropwise. The temperature was raised to 120 °C and the reaction was carried out for 5 h till the methylolation reaction came to an end, the completion of the reaction was noticed by noting down the acid value and free formaldehyde content every hour. The product was dried at 60 °C for 48 h in the oven. The purity of the product was determined by free phenol content at the end of the reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CBN resin using ultrasound irradiation method (US)
ce:section.ce:para: Carrying out the reaction by utilising ultrasonic energy was a simpler procedure. Ultrasonic horn of 22 kHz was used to carry out the reaction (Fig. 1). For a comparative study between conventional reaction and ultrasonic reaction, the same amount of cardanol, formaldehyde, citric acid and methanol were taken which is mentioned in the conventional heating method. The reaction was carried out for one hour and the temperature of the reaction mixture was maintained between 25 and 30 °C by adding ice in the water bath. The temperature of the reaction mixture was monitored using the thermometer. Acid value and free formaldehyde content were noted every 10 min. In a similar manner, purity of the product was found out. pH was monitored for both the reactions.

Plethora experiments were performed in order to study the synthesis of CBN resin using the ultrasonic horn and optimise parameters such as reaction time, reactant ratio, catalyst loading, type of acid catalyst and intensity of the ultrasound. The parameters were optimised based on the free formaldehyde content, acid value, viscosity and yield of the produced.

The reaction time required for the completion of the reaction was optimised using acid value titration method. Also, the free formaldehyde content, viscosity and the pH of the product were evaluated for the optimisation of the time of the reaction. Brookfield Viscometer was used to measure the viscosity of the cardanol based novolac resin. pH meter of Arvind Industries model SV3T was used to check the pH of CBN resin.

Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FTIR) Spectroscopy was used for the confirmation of the product and operated at Attenuated Total Reflection (ATR) mode and scanning was carried out in the range of 800–4000 cm−1. The spectrum was recorded using the model Shimadzu Japan (8400S). Agilent 1260 Infinity GPC was used for the Gel Permeation Chromatography analysis of CBN resin for the molecular weight confirmation. The elemental analysis was carried out using Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy 1H NMR spectrum of the polymer was recorded at 500 MHz. NMR grade CDCl3 was used as a solvent. 0.7 g of CBN was dissolved in 1 ml of CDCl3. Sample temperature was stabilised at 25 °C. Agilent 500 MHz, which had a highly homogeneous superconducting magnet, was used for analysing the samples. The synthesised CBN resin was subjected to Thermo Gravimetric Analysis (TGA) analysis with thermogravimetric analyser, Perkin-Elmer (Model Pyris 1 TGA). 15 mg of the sample was taken, sample purge was 50 ml/min and balance purge was 140 ml/min. The heating rate was kept as 10 °C/min in the presence of nitrogen atmosphere. The resin was heated from 30 °C to 500 °C. TGA was performed before curing of the resin.

Cardanol polymerizes either by unsaturation in the side chain or by a condensation reaction with formaldehyde. The degree of unsaturation of cardanol was 317 and that of CBN resin was also found to be 317 as it was. There was no change in the degree of unsaturation or iodine value before and after the reaction entailing that no reaction occurred at the alkyl chain of the cardanol. The reaction between cardanol and formaldehyde in presence of an acid catalyst is a step-growth polymerization. The mechanism involves the formation of novolac oligomers, in acidic media using formaldehyde and excess of cardanol which shown in section 2.2. Hydroxymethylene carbonium ion from methylene glycol is formed in the first step, which acts as a hydroxyl alkylating agent. Under acidic conditions, methylol group acts as transient intermediate; this, in turn, leads to the formation of cardanylic carbonium ion. Also, due to the instability of the methylol group looses water molecule readily. The cardanylic carbonium ion reacts with cardanol instantaneously yielding dihydroxydicardanal [4].

The time required for the completion of the reaction was optimised based on free formaldehyde content, acid value, viscosity and pH. The formylation reaction was carried out with 0.5 mol of formaldehyde and 1 mol of cardanol in the presence of citric acid as a catalyst. For both, the conventionally synthesised product and ultrasonically synthesised product, pH was closely monitored. In both the cases, pH of the initial reaction mixture was 5.7. The pH of the reaction mass was decreased as the reaction proceeded. pH of reaction mixture was found to be 2.3 at the end of reaction. Monohydroxyl substituted cardanol could be the reason for a fall in pH of the reaction mixture [28]. Periodic evaluation of free formaldehyde content and the estimation of the acid value were done in order to check the completion of methylolation reaction. Complete methylolation reaction could produce a CBN resin with high ortho-ortho linkages.

From Fig. 2 , one can see that after 5 h of reaction time by a conventional heating method, the free formaldehyde content in the reaction mixture was 6.11%. This means that 95.89% of the formaldehyde had been reacted after 5 h, which marked the end of the methylolation reaction; pH of the mixture at this point was 2.3 and the acid value was 31.7. The viscosity of the CBN resin after drying was 83.3 cP. Free formaldehyde content of the product was found to be 6.11% at the end of 5 h of reaction time at 120 °C temperature.

While carrying out the reaction with ultrasound assistance (at room temperature), the desired pH of 2.3 was achieved within 30 min of the reaction time (Fig. 3 ). The reaction time was reduced owing to macromolecular mixing of acoustic irradiation and also due to the local hot spot generated during the acoustic irradiation. From Fig. 4 , it can be observed that least free formaldehyde content and the highest acid value was obtained when the reaction time was kept as 30 min. In Fig. 5 , the highest viscosity of the product was observed when the reaction time was kept as 30 min. After 30 min of reaction time, free formaldehyde content started increasing, acid value started decreasing and viscosity value started decreasing. While using ultrasound, there is an optimum time beyond which polymer would start degrading. From this study, it can be observed that physical effects of ultrasound can initiate and accelerate the step-growth polymerization and at some point, the polymer starts degrading due to molecular bond breakdown. Many studies have reported ultrasonic irradiation (acoustic cavitation) as a technique of depolymerization capable of reducing the molecular weight of the polymer [29–31]. This is the reason behind the decrease in viscosity after 30 min of reaction time. When the polymer degrades or splits, some of the reactants come back into the mixture, therefore there is a noticeable enhancement in the free formaldehyde content beyond 30 min of reaction time. Considering these factors for the next set of parameters, the reaction time was kept as 30 min. Another point to be noted is, all the three factors (free formaldehyde content, acid value and viscosity) almost coincided at the reaction time of 30 min. To optimise other parameters instead of considering all the three factors, the only viscosity was considered as controlling criteria. pH of the reaction mixture after half hour remained constant (Fig. 3).

The amount of energy saved could be realised when compared to the conventional synthesis. After 30 min of reaction time, free formaldehyde content and viscosity (after drying) was found to be 0.38% and 61.8 cP (Fig. 5) respectively. This entails that most of the formaldehyde has been consumed in the reaction and only 0.38% of formaldehyde has been left unreacted; this certainly marks the end of the methylolation reaction. The viscosities of product synthesised through both the pathways are also comparable, though the ultrasound showed a slightly lower value indicating a possible reduction in the average molecular weight of the formed polymer, possibly due to acoustic cavitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimisation of cardanol to formaldehyde ratio
ce:section.ce:para: As the ratio of formaldehyde to cardanol increased, a decrease in the viscosity was observed (Fig. 6 ), which is not desirable. From the reaction scheme 1, it could be seen that 2 moles of cardanol reacted with 1 mol of formaldehyde to form 1 mol of CBN monomer. Therefore, 1: 0.5-mol ratio of cardanol to formaldehyde was yielding the best result. One of the interpretations may be, as the formaldehyde ratio increased, the amount of unreacted formaldehyde in the reaction mixture increased leading to a drop in the viscosity values. This could also be the condensation reaction at multiple locations leading to lowering of the average molecular weight. For all the reactions, pH of the reaction mixture at the end of 30 min was found to be 2.2. For the next set of reactions, cardanol to formaldehyde mole ratio was kept as 1:0.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimisation of cardanol to catalyst ratio
ce:section.ce:para: The amount of catalyst (citric acid) was varied from 0.15% to 7.5% of the weight of the cardanol. When the citric acid amount was too low (0.15% & 0.33%) desired pH and the viscosity was not achieved in the 30 min reaction time (Fig. 7 ). Since pH did not decrease much (5.7–5.5), it indicated that good methylolation reaction did not occur. It could be interpreted that at very low catalyst concentration, the reaction did not occur completely and the catalyst did not suffice for the reaction. Desired pH and viscosity values were attained when the catalyst weight was 2.5% and more. As the catalyst quantity was increased, the viscosity and the pH value remained almost constant. After an optimum catalyst weight percentage (2.5%), the reaction was not influenced by any enhancement in the catalyst amount.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different catalysts on the CBN
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the type of the catalyst on the product was checked using different catalysts as shown in Fig. 8 . When strong acids were used, the viscosity of the product was 4 to 5 times higher (400 to 500 Cp from 80 to 150 Cp) and pH was also much lower as compared to the product synthesised in the presence of weak acids as a catalyst. Therefore, product synthesised in presence of strong acids is desirable. Coming to weak organic acids, pKa values of adipic acid, citric acid and oxalic acid are in decreasing order (4.19, 2.79 and 1.15 respectively) and so is their viscosity. From the data, it can be interpreted that higher the acid strength, higher is the power to influence the methylolation reaction. This is evident from the viscosity values and pH at the end of the reaction (Fig. 8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of power on the CBN
ce:section.ce:para:
Hydrochloric acid was used as a catalyst. The wattage (power dissipation) of the ultrasonic horn was variable. Current utilised by the horn was displayed and hence power supplied to the horn was calculated as by Eq. (1),
(1)
P = V × I,
Where

ce:list-item.ce:para: ‘I’ is the current consumed by the horn.

ce:section.ce:para: The electrical to mechanical efficiency of the horn was calorimetrically evaluated to be 15%. The reaction time was initially optimised as 30 min.

E, Energy per unit reactant volume supplied to the horn was calculated as by Eq. (2),
(2)
E = P × 0.2 ×
t
v
Where

ce:list-item.ce:para: ‘v’ is the volume of the reaction mixture.

ce:section.ce:para: Table 1 depicts the dependence of viscosity on the energy supplied to the horn. Viscosity initially increased and then decreased. pH of the reaction mixture was 1.3 at the end of 30 min for all the five reactions. It can be observed that for a particular reaction time if excess energy is supplied then viscosity starts decreasing. Intense acoustic cavitation may degrade the polymer or oligomers formed as was discussed earlier. An optimum energy per ml of the reaction mixture, for this system, was found to be 178.5 J/ml.

The appearance of different peaks in Figs. 9
and 10 indicate the characteristic peaks of pure cardanol and ultrasonically synthesised CBN resin indicating the in both the cases the mechanism appears to be the same. The position of peaks in figures was found to similar to the CBN resins synthesised earlier in the previous studies [8]. The most important functional group in the CBN structure is the methylene group (–CH2–), linked between two benzene rings and it was revealed by the appearance of the peak at 1350 cm−1 (Fig. 10). In methylolated cardanol, there is a CO stretch from CH2OH. This was evident from the shift of a peak from 1078.9 (Fig. 9) to 1102.1 cm−1 (Fig. 10). The ortho and para substitution at the benzene nuclei were realised by the appearance of a peak at near 721.4 (Fig. 10). The intensity of peaks at 777.8 cm−1 (CH out of plane deformation) 1591.2 cm−1 (CC, stretch), 3009 cm−1 (CH stretch of alkene) was not affected, entailing polymerization did not take place through double bonds in the side chain and polymerization happened through substitution of CH2OH. The small peaks near 912 cm−1 and 693.8 cm−1 indicated the presence of three adjacent hydrogen atoms in the benzene nuclei. This spectral data was found similar to the earlier studies of CBN resins [4,32].
The final product was analysed by GPC analysis, a product which was synthesised after optimising all the parameters in US method. GPC chromatogram of the CBN resin is depicted in Fig. 11 .

For a step-growth polymerization, the Carothers equation gives the degree of polymerization, X n, for a given fractional monomer conversion, p. When a monomer is used in excess molar ratio, the Carothers equation is given as,
(3)
Xn =
1 + r
1 + r - 2 rp

The above Eq. (3) is for linear monomers. ‘r’ is the stoichiometric ratio and is less than 1. From free formaldehyde content, the extent of the reaction (p) was found to be 99%. Stoichiometric ratio ‘r’ was 0.5. From the Eq. (3), the degree of polymerization X n was calculated. The value of X n was 2.94, entailing that there was 2.94 number of monomer units in the CBN polymer. Therefore, it is indicating the formation of a trimer. The structure of CBN resin is linear and therefore the average chain length was calculated using the following Eq. (4) given by Podzimek and Hroch [33].
(4)
v =
12 +
M
n
312

The value of Mn (=1225 g/mol) was obtained through GPC. The average kinetic chain length was calculated to be 3.96. Tiwari et al. (2011) synthesised CBN resin with the aid of microwave irradiation and the average kinetic chain length was found to be 1.64 [34]. An ideal step-growth polymerization has a polydispersity (PD) of 2 (Carothers' equation limits PD values of linear step-growth polymers to 2 and below). PD is the distribution of individual molecular mass in a batch of polymers. PD is defined as the ratio of Mw to Mn. The value of PD of the synthesised CBN is 1.968 indicating a narrow distribution of molecular mass and ideal step-growth polymerization. Data obtained from GPC is depicted in Table 2 .

The 1H NMR spectral data of the synthesised CBN resin was found to be similar to that given in the literature [4,35]. In Fig. 12 , a peak at 0.9 δ indicated the presence of terminal methyl group of the alkyl side chain. The peaks at 1.3 δ and 2.6 δ, indicated the presence of long (>five methylene groups) aliphatic side chain, these peaks are originally present in the cardanol. A peak at 3.8 δ could be attributed to the presence of methylene protons of C6H5-CH2-C6H5, which act as a bridge between two phenyl rings. The peak at 5.5 δ appeared due to the presence of methylene protons. The peak at 6.8 δ could be due to the presence of aryl protons of benzene nuclei. The presence of peaks at 7.1–7.3 δ might be due to the phenolic hydroxyl in the CBN resin. All these spectral data of CBN resin entail that the condensation of methylolated cardanol is completely accomplished.

TGA of the uncured resin confirmed the process of degradation of the sample. The degradation of the resin occurred in four steps (Fig. 13 ). Fig. 13 showed the TGA scans of cardanol based novolac resin. The uncured polymer sample was analysed for its thermal stability by TGA trace resulted from thermo gravimetric analyser (TGA) at a heating rate of 10 °C in a nitrogen atmosphere. The sample was stable up to 232 °C and started losing weight above this temperature. The initial decomposition occurred at 232 °C which is comparable to the uncured phenolic resin [36]. It has been reported that the decomposition of the cardanol occurs at 280 °C at around 85% [37]. In case of synthesised CBN resin at 280 °C only 30% of decomposition observed. From this data, it is clear that the thermal stability of the synthesised CBN is better than the cardanol. Rapid decomposition was observed in the temperature range of 422–466 °C and almost total volatilization of the samples occurred around 500 °C.

An energy efficient, simple and green approach has been developed successfully for the synthesis of novolac resin. From the experiments, data and analysis it can be elucidated that energy from the ultrasonic horn can be utilised to synthesise CBN resin. It can be observed that there was a huge reduction in the reaction time when ultrasound was used which reduced from 5 h (300 min) to 30 min, the amount of time and energy saved can be realised. It is for the first time that ultrasound has been used to synthesise CBN resin at room temperature, however, scale-up studies are yet to be done.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to the Department of Science and Technology, Government of India, for providing the financial assistance for the research work.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: D. Wasser-mm, C.R. Dawson, Cashew nutshell liquid, (n.d.) 396–399.
M.T. Harvey, M. Operations, Cashew Nut Shell Liquid, Engineering. 32 (n.d.) 1306–1310.

Ultrasound: A boon in the synthesis of organic compounds
Sonochemistry and sonoprocessing: the link, the trends and (probably) the future
